ale: noun. A beer made without hops.
alee: adverb. On the lee side of a ship, to the leeward side (vs aweather)
all: determiner. Every individual or anything of the given class, with no exceptions (the noun or noun phrase denoting the class must be plural
allee: noun. A tree-lined avenue, often particularly one that is part of a landscaped garden.
are: verb. second-person singular simple present of be
arf: interjection. Synonym of woof (“sound of a dog's bark”).
awe: noun. A feeling of fear and reverence.
awl: noun. A pointed instrument for piercing small holes, as in leather or wood; used by shoemakers, saddlers, cabinetmakers, etc.
ear: noun. The organ of hearing, consisting of the pinna or auricle, auditory canal, eardrum, malleus, incus, stapes and cochlea.
earl: noun. A British or Irish nobleman next in rank above a viscount and below a marquess; equivalent to a European count.
eel: noun. Any freshwater fish of the order Anguilliformes, which are elongated and resemble snakes.
elf: noun. A supernatural being or spirit associated with illness, mischief, and harmful or dangerous magical influence; in later Norse sources,
era: noun. A time period of indeterminate length, generally more than one year.
ewe: noun. A female sheep, as opposed to a ram.
ewer: noun. A kind of widemouthed pitcher or jug with a shape like a vase and a handle, and originally used for carrying water.
fare: noun. A going; journey; travel; voyage; course; passage.
farewell: noun. A wish of happiness or safety at parting, especially a permanent departure.
farl: noun. A quarter of a thin oatmeal or flour cake.
fear: noun. A strong, unpleasant emotion or feeling caused by actual or perceived danger or threat.
fee: noun. An amount charged for a privilege.
feel: verb. To use or experience the sense of touch.
fell: verb. To make something fall; especially to chop down a tree.
feller: noun. A person who fells trees; a lumberjack
fere: noun. A companion, comrade or friend.
few: determiner. An indefinite, but usually small, number of.
fewer: determiner. comparative degree of few; a smaller number.
flaw: noun. A flake, fragment, or shiver.
flea: noun. A small, wingless, parasitic insect of the order Siphonaptera, renowned for its bloodsucking habits and jumping abilities.
flee: verb. To run away; to escape.
fleer: verb. To make a wry face in contempt, or to grin in scorn
lar: noun. singular of lares: a household god, particularly overseeing the family itself.
law: noun. The body of binding rules and regulations, customs, and standards established in a community by its legislative and judicial
lea: noun. An open field, meadow, pasture.
leaf: noun. The usually green and flat organ that represents the most prominent feature of most vegetative plants.
lee: noun. A protected cove or harbor, out of the wind.
leer: verb. To look sideways or obliquely; now especially with sexual desire or malicious intent.
rale: noun. An abnormal clicking, rattling or crackling sound, made by one or both lungs and heard with a stethoscope, caused by the popping open
real: adjective. True, genuine, not merely nominal or apparent.
ree: noun. Alternative form of rei (“Portuguese real”).
reef: noun. A chain or range of rocks, sand, or coral lying at or near the surface of the water.
reel: noun. A shaky or unsteady gait.
refel: verb. To refute, disprove (an argument); to confute (someone).
refell: verb. simple past and past participle of refall
wafer: noun. A light, thin, flat biscuit / cookie.
wale: noun. A ridge or low barrier.
waler: noun. A breed of light saddle horse from Australia, once favoured as a warhorse.
wall: noun. A rampart of earth, stones etc. built up for defensive purposes.
war: noun. Organized, large-scale, armed conflict between countries or between national, ethnic, or other sizeable groups, usually but not
ware: noun. Goods or a type of goods offered for sale or use.
wear: verb. To have on: To carry or have equipped on or about one's body, as an item of clothing, equipment, decoration, perfume, etc.
weel: noun. A trap for catching fish; a weely.
welfare: noun. Health, safety, happiness and prosperity; well-being in any respect.
were: verb. second-person singular simple past indicative of be
afer: adjective. African (Used as a specific epithet).
alf: noun. A supernatural being similar to an elf; one of the Dǫkkálfar or Ljósalfar.
areel: adjective. Reeling; spinning or moving unsteadily, especially from shock, surprise, or confusion.
arew: adverb. Obsolete spelling of arow (“in a row”).
feer: adjective. Alternative form of fear (“able, capable”).
fra: noun. A title of a friar or monk: brother.
lew: noun. A French gold coin circulated in 15th- century Scotland.
rew: noun. Obsolete or dialectal form of row
rewall: verb. To provide with new walls.
waf: interjection. Represents the sound of a fox barking.
weller: adjective. comparative form of well: more well
wer: noun. A man; a male; a husband.
